title: Being Okay. Essential Knowledge About Mental Health

chapters.title: When the body signals distress
chapters.text:
We are taught from childhood that health ranks among life's highest goods — almost a blessing rather than continuous work spanning nutrition, movement, scheduling, and intellectual development.

Another downside of that view is how society elevates physical health while sidelining mental strain. Many people hide psychological struggles even from themselves, dismissing hardship with phrases like "That's life," "It's PMS," "Just a bad day," or "I'll rest and feel fine." When one rough day becomes two, or low mood persists regardless of cycle or sleep, the brain may be signaling distress. Warning signs include:

• Apathy toward work, relationships, or hobbies
• Low productivity, intrusive thoughts, poor focus
• Weight swings or disrupted eating
• Loss of libido
• Broken sleep patterns
• Depressed mood, irritability, or suicidal thoughts
• Reliance on caffeine, alcohol, nicotine, or sugar
• Persistent anxiety

If symptoms last more than two weeks, seek professional help. Begin with a family doctor, who assembles medical history, evaluates causes, and recommends specialists. Depressed mood can stem from physical illness, and long-term psychological strain can trigger somatic disease. Treatment may require combined approaches.

Understand whom you are seeing:

• Psychiatrists address neurological functioning and neurotransmitters.
• Psychotherapists work on beliefs, self-image, and childhood trauma.
• Psychologists handle non-medical life issues such as conflict, values, or career decisions.

Seeking care is not shameful. Cultural stereotypes about psychiatric coercion are exaggerated; forced hospitalization without legal grounds is illegal. Moreover, cultural stereotypes about mental disorders are a hyperbole.

In this summary, we will discuss the anatomy and functioning of the human brain and uncover the biochemical processes behind mental health issues. We will also gain basic knowledge about common disorders so you can recognize when professional support may help. Sometimes treatment requires a combination of medical and therapeutic approaches tailored to your situation. Your family doctor will evaluate your symptoms and recommend a specialist when appropriate.

chapters.title: Inside the brain's shared emotional quarters
chapters.text:
The brain is the command center regulating every aspect of life. Psychological processes begin and develop here, so understanding basic function clarifies the psyche.

The brain and body are closely linked; changes in one ripple through the other.

Good physical health supports mental well-being and vice versa.

Thoughts and emotions do not occupy separate rooms. Joy, rage, euphoria, and sadness share a crowded dormitory, regulated collaboratively across brain regions and bodily signals. The limbic system acts as chief emotional manager, receiving, processing, and routing information like software. The hypothalamus connects body, brain, and feeling, governing hunger, sexual behavior, blood sugar, and interoception — awareness of internal states.

The amygdala reacts first to stimuli, encoding external events into emotional responses before other structures weigh in. It is the critical coder, transforming external signals into emotions.

The nervous system is generally complex and consists of two main parts. It splits into central (brain and spinal cord) and peripheral branches (sympathetic, parasympathetic, enteric). Peripheral networks automate many processes without conscious effort. The sympathetic system mobilizes fight, flight, or freeze when resources must be redirected fast.

If you struggle with anxiety or stress, sympathetic activation may run continuously.

The parasympathetic system restores calm. However, the impact of a restless amygdala can disrupt the normal functioning of all organs. The classic example of the body-mind link is vegetative dystonia. This diagnosis refers to any issues with blood pressure regulation or a disruption in the autonomous nervous system. But this disease does not exist in any country except the post-Soviet ones. It usually means a doctor does not wish to find the nervous system issues that damage your cardiovascular health. Regulating feelings is a collaboration between many areas of the brain and the body's signals.

Did you know? Ancient Greeks believed the human soul lived in the hypothalamus.

chapters.title: Neurons, synapses, and plasticity
chapters.text:
Brain and nerves run on electrically active cells called neurons that transmit information chemically and electrically. Neurons extend short dendrites and long axons, requiring glucose and ketones — which is why the body demands carbohydrates. A blood-brain barrier blocks many molecules, complicating treatment when amino acids cannot reach neural tissue.

Connection points are synapses. When electrical charge arrives, neurons release neurotransmitters — neuromediators — that carry commands forward.

Major neurotransmitters include:

• Glutamate, driving excitation
• Dopamine, influencing motivation, decision-making, perception, and sexual or eating behavior
• Serotonin, necessary for stable energy

Disruption in the functioning and balance of neuromediators is the core of all mental illnesses.

Neural networks resemble social webs: relationships strengthen with regular contact and weaken without it. Throughout life we build a web of ties that branch like dendrites. Neurons grow connections with hundreds of partners, yet inactive links fade, changing the entire system — much like friendships that dissolve without communication.

That adaptive rewiring — neuroplasticity — enables learning, environmental adjustment, recovery after injury, and response to stress. The rapid adaptability of a neural network to external and internal factors is what makes recovery and growth possible even after significant disruption.

The nervous system is a complex, dynamic network that develops, learns, and communicates within itself. As you can see, the work of a neural network is very similar to the development of human relationships and contacts.

chapters.title: Roots of psychological difficulty
chapters.text:
Mental health problems rarely have a single cause. Common contributors include:

Genetic predisposition. Specific variants of receptor genes and dopamine or serotonin transporters can load the gun, but environment pulls the trigger, exciting and deepening inclinations. Previous generations are not a verdict; neuroplasticity lets you rewrite the program.

Difficult childhood. Children facing chronic early stress from neglect, abusive parenting, or bullying face elevated risk. Childhood trauma is not a life sentence, yet overcoming it usually requires qualified professional help.

Personality traits. Some theorists treat stress as a side effect of inherent qualities rather than sudden external shocks. Rumination and invalidating anything positive can produce anhedonia — inability to feel pleasure despite objective reasons for joy. Such tendencies arise from society, information flow, parents, and life events. One negative situation can provoke many others when mood colors interpretation.

Physical health. Long-term disease harms mental health in two ways: biochemical disruption of neural interaction and psychological depression caused by ongoing stress of illness.

Poverty. Limited financial resources increase risk through poor medicine access, nutrition, sleep, work-life imbalance, and addictions. Stereotypes that healthy food and therapy belong only to the rich exaggerate helplessness; proactive attitudes still matter. The wealthy get sick too. Therefore, a more proactive attitude is vital to improving your life even when resources are constrained.

Affordable foods can be nutritious when you compare labels carefully.

Traumatic experiences. Do not assume trauma requires war, accidents, or disasters. Virtually any everyday situation can exceed your endurance and wound you. Most triggers lie outside your control, yet accepting that trauma can cause mental illness is the first step toward repair. You cannot avoid every traumatic event since most are out of your control. Instead, you must accept that trauma causes mental illness and start working on improving your health. Genetics only load the gun; environment and circumstances pull the trigger.

chapters.title: Depression's chemistry and subtypes
chapters.text:
Depression is so common we sometimes normalize it alongside coffee, social media, and television. If you or someone you know shows the following symptoms — and they are not solely due to addiction, substance abuse, medication, or another mood disorder — seek help:

• Depressed mood
• Indifference, inability to feel joy or satisfaction
• Weight fluctuations and appetite problems
• Poor sleep schedule
• Inhibition or constant restlessness
• Exhaustion, as if an internal battery reached zero
• Concentration and clear-thinking difficulties
• Suicidal thoughts or intentions

Typically, depression is triggered by disrupted environments (stress, trauma, or nutrition) and genetics that increase enzymes destroying neurotransmitters. Both factors malfunction neural networks. Destroyed neurotransmitters leave the brain short on dopamine, serotonin, norepinephrine, and GABA. Without serotonin, anxiety circuits overexcite while pleasure and motivation circuits stall. Enzyme levels rise and neurons lose GABA, the inhibitory mediator that filters stimuli and helps us calm down.

Major depressive disorder has subtypes:

• Persistent depressive disorder — depressed state lasting at least two years with alternating severe and mild episodes
• Postpartum depression — dangerous major-depressive symptoms after childbirth
• Bipolar disorder — depression alternating with mania or hypomania
• Premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD) — psychiatric reaction to sex hormones, distinct from PMS; nearly five percent of people who menstruate are affected
• Seasonal affective disorder (SAD) — chronic depression appearing at a particular season due to melatonin-serotonin imbalance, often within major depressive or bipolar illness

The above symptoms and chemical reactions in the brain are related to major depressive disorder. Still, this illness has subtypes that require tailored understanding even when symptoms overlap.

Fortunately, all types are treatable. The main methods are antidepressants (medication that uses chemical compounds to help improve brain function), psychotherapy (cognitive-behavioral or interpersonal), brain stimulation (transcranial, magnetic, vagus, or electroconvulsive), and lifestyle changes encouraging physical activity, proper nutrition, socialization, and limiting harmful habits. Due to the destruction of neurotransmitters, the brain lacks the chemical balance needed for calm and motivation until treatment restores equilibrium.

chapters.title: Anxiety disorders in a turbulent world
chapters.text:
Depression shares the spotlight with anxiety and its subtypes — especially salient amid the COVID-19 pandemic, political conflict, and war.

Worry during real danger is normal. Anxiety becomes clinical when fear persists without threat or appears for no apparent reason. The amygdala flags danger; a healthy brain and prefrontal cortex downgrade minor risks and settle quickly. In anxiety disorders, an oversensitive amygdala overestimates threat, breaks free from cortical control, and spirals into catastrophic fantasies. Prolonged anxiety erodes plasticity, destroys hippocampal neurons, and bleaches positive memories from view. You begin seeing the world through a catastrophically pessimistic lens.

Anxiety also disturbs digestion and can trigger gastroenterological symptoms.

Like depression, there are a few types of anxiety disorder:

Common forms include generalized anxiety disorder — the inability to control everyday things — phobias, social anxiety disorder, panic disorder, health anxiety disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder, substance-induced anxiety, and adjustment disorder with anxiety.

Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) arises from tragic events such as war, violence, or death. Severe stress can malfunction the endocrine system and reconfigure neural networks so structures lose the ability to assess threat adequately. Dopamine and norepinephrine rise while serotonin and GABA fall, triggering terrible memories and associations in response to the slightest cue. Pictures of the past interfere with everyday life.

Considering recent events in Ukraine, let's look at another type of anxiety — post-traumatic stress disorder. War, violence, death, or severe stress can cause a malfunction of the endocrine system and reconfiguration of the neural networks. These structures lose the ability to assess and respond to the threat adequately.

Consulting a specialist is the first step to overcoming PTSD and other types of anxiety. They can determine the exact diagnosis and detect any accompanying psychiatric or somatic disorders. You might opt for antidepressants but do not dismiss cognitive-behavioral therapy too. Explore self-help options and begin improving your mental state. Anxiety increases due to an imbalance in the levels of hormones and neurotransmitters (dopamine and norepinephrine increase, and serotonin and GABA decrease). The brain triggers terrible memories and associations in response to the slightest threat. Pictures of the past always appear in a person's mind, interfering with their everyday life until treatment interrupts the cycle.

chapters.title: When eating becomes the battleground
chapters.text:
By now you have probably noticed that eating disorders appear both as symptoms of broader mental illness and as primary conditions requiring direct care. Food saturates the body with nutrients, yet some disorders center appetite itself — notably anorexia and bulimia.

Anorexia involves dangerously low body mass index driven by food restriction, laxative abuse, or excessive physical training.

Bulimia features regular overeating followed by purging or other compensatory behaviors, often accompanied by compulsive shopping, drug or alcohol abuse, and self-harm.

The brain of a person with anorexia discourages consumption, so they are not prone to drug or alcohol addictions in the same way.

In this section, we will talk about mental disorders centered around eating behavior, including anorexia and bulimia. Despite beliefs that unhealthy beauty standards and patriarchal pressure cause these diseases, scientists say the primary factors are heredity, poorly developed interoception (difficulty sensing hunger or fullness), and inability to withstand certain emotional states. Defense mechanisms block unpleasant emotions through refusal of food, compulsive overeating, or stress eating.

People with anorexia are often withdrawn, lonely, anxious, and unable to feel joy. Their brains are susceptible to threats and receive no pleasure from food. They usually hide their condition. Anorexia is deeply rooted in neural networks, making recovery arduous. Bulimia, which also often appears in adolescence, is sometimes caused by parental neglect that deprived oxytocin release during bonding, so limbic networks form incorrectly and hinder self-regulation.

Both diseases negatively affect health, destroy the body, and can cause numerous accompanying illnesses.

Overcoming these disorders requires a comprehensive approach, including mental and physical treatment. A gradual return to healthy nutrition, restoration of the digestive system, consultations with a psychotherapist, cognitive-behavioral therapy, and exercise are necessary. Support from friends and family can make the process easier and smoother.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Mental health is not dark matter. Scientific progress has made this branch of medicine as accessible for treatment and regulation as other parts of the human body. A psychiatric disorder is not a life sentence. Sometimes it is okay to be not okay or to need help.

Stigma and horror stories about antidepressants do not align with reality. Modern medications do not shoot blindly; they regulate disrupted neurotransmitter communication, increasing or decreasing levels and repairing vital receptors. Drug treatment is not mandatory — in many cases therapy plus healthy diet and lifestyle improve the situation without medication.

People with mental disorders are not monsters, crazy, or hopeless. Depression, anxiety, and eating disorders can happen to anyone, and a supportive environment is critical to recovery.

Collective trauma matters too. During Ukraine's history its people experienced extermination, conquest, famine, banned language and culture, and rewritten history. Yet they survived. Somewhere deep in their genes the pain of past generations was imprinted alongside an indomitable spirit. Not only Ukrainians, but all people must work through historical and cultural traumas on a national scale — not from the position of a victim but a survivor and fighter. Then we can all become healthier and more aware.

Try this

Be kind to yourself and your loved ones. Observe and pay attention to changes in mood, ideas, and narratives that dominate your circle of acquaintances and family. If you notice alarming symptoms, support them through a difficult period and seek professional help if you are struggling.

Read it till the end?
